primary_diagnosis.name: Acute cholecystitis (calculous), exacerbation on a background of known gallstones

pathophysiology_summary: A gallstone has become persistently impacted in the gallbladder neck or cystic duct. Unlike previous episodes of biliary colic — where the stone dislodged and the transient obstruction resolved within a few hours — the sustained obstruction has caused gallbladder wall inflammation, distension, mural oedema and secondary bacterial infection, producing acute (calculous) cholecystitis. The prolonged (>6 hours) continuous right-upper-quadrant pain, fever and localised peritonism (Murphy's sign, RUQ guarding) reflect established localised inflammation rather than transient spasm. Untreated, the process can progress to gallbladder empyema, gangrene and perforation, or ascend to cause cholangitis and biliary sepsis. Definitive diagnosis and severity grading (Tokyo Guidelines TG18) require abdominal ultrasound plus inflammatory markers and liver function tests — none of which are obtainable via telehealth.

differential.evidence_basis: Prolonged continuous RUQ pain radiating to the right scapula in a patient with known gallstones, after a fatty meal, with fever.
The known pattern — but biliary colic is transient (usually <6 hours) and is NOT accompanied by fever; the duration and fever argue against it.
Empty antacid packets and prior self-medication raise dyspepsia — but the pain is RUQ radiating to the scapula rather than epigastric burning relieved by antacids.
Fever plus RUQ pain raises cholangitis; must screen for jaundice, rigors and confusion (Charcot's triad / Reynolds' pentad).
Gallstones can precipitate pancreatitis; severe upper abdominal pain with vomiting warrants exclusion with lipase — not obtainable remotely.
Can refer pain to the RUQ with fever; less likely with no cough/breathlessness, but cannot be auscultated remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, acute cholecystitis leads but cholangitis, pancreatitis and a surgical/vascular mimic cannot be excluded remotely — the safe move is urgent in-person assessment, not remote confirmation.

differential.evidence_basis: Prolonged pain + fever + prior gallstones + inspiratory catch, with no jaundice/dark urine/pale stool — pattern of established cholecystitis without biliary obstruction.
No jaundice or confusion reported, making cholangitis less likely — but fever keeps it on the list until bloods/imaging exclude it.
Duration >6 hours with fever is incompatible with simple transient colic.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct telehealth conclusion is a presumptive acute biliary/surgical-abdomen picture requiring urgent in-person evaluation — NOT a confirmed diagnosis and NOT reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: Clinician exam (positive Murphy's sign, RUQ guarding), fever and tachycardia confirm localised inflammation; ultrasound (anticipated thickened gallbladder wall) plus bloods complete the Tokyo-Guidelines diagnosis in hospital.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final confirmation is a hospital determination (exam + ultrasound + bloods); the AI Doctor's ceiling is a well-reasoned presumptive suspicion driving urgent escalation.

red_flags.description: Continuous right-upper-quadrant pain persisting beyond 6 hours in a patient whose prior attacks were self-limiting
red_flags.clinical_significance: Duration beyond 6 hours marks the transition from transient biliary colic to established gallbladder inflammation (acute cholecystitis).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-calling as another colic attack delays surgical assessment; risk of empyema, gangrene or perforation.

red_flags.description: Fever with chills and tachycardia — systemic inflammatory response
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic inflammation signals infection/sepsis risk and mandates urgent in-person assessment, IV antibiotics and bloods.

red_flags.description: Intractable vomiting with inability to maintain hydration
red_flags.clinical_significance: Dehydration and inability to take oral intake require IV fluids and support the need for hospital care; also mandates nil-by-mouth in case of surgery.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Worsening dehydration, electrolyte disturbance, delayed surgical preparation.

red_flags.description: Positive Murphy's sign with localised RUQ guarding and rigidity (clinician-elicited)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Localised peritonism confirms established gallbladder inflammation; this is a clinician examination finding that cannot be elicited via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: The AI cannot reproduce this sign — over-reliance on remote assessment risks false reassurance.

red_flags.description: Warning features of progression — generalised/rigid abdomen (perforation), haematemesis/melaena, jaundice with rigors or confusion (cholangitis), or faintness/shock
red_flags.clinical_significance: None present at contact, but each is a time-critical deterioration edge the patient/carer must be told to act on immediately (000).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to safety-net a perforation, biliary sepsis or GI haemorrhage.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the pain to 'another biliary colic attack' because the patient has known gallstones, and missing that the prolonged duration plus fever now indicate established cholecystitis.
Reassuring and advising home management (antacids, simple analgesia, wait it out) instead of escalating a febrile surgical abdomen for urgent in-person assessment.
Failing to screen for jaundice, rigors, hypotension and confusion — the features that would upgrade cholangitis and biliary sepsis to an immediate 000 emergency.
Being led by the empty antacid packets to call this gastritis/reflux and under-triage.
Trying to confirm or exclude cholecystitis remotely — it cannot be diagnosed or a surgical/vascular mimic excluded without an abdominal examination, bloods and ultrasound.

risk_scores.tool_name: Tokyo Guidelines TG18 (acute cholecystitis diagnostic criteria)
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Suspected/definite acute cholecystitis pattern (local signs of inflammation + systemic signs of inflammation); severity grading requires hospital bloods/imaging
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Meets clinical criteria for suspected acute cholecystitis; definitive grading and imaging are hospital-based.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Murphy's sign (inspiratory arrest on subcostal palpation)
RUQ guarding and rigidity / degree of localised peritonism
Blood pressure and heart rate without a home device
Full blood count, CRP, liver function tests, lipase, blood cultures
Abdominal ultrasound (gallbladder wall thickness, pericholecystic fluid, stones)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the acute febrile biliary / surgical-abdomen pattern and arrange URGENT same-day in-person assessment at an emergency department for examination, bloods, imaging (ultrasound) and surgical review; advise nil by mouth; escalate immediately to 000 if generalised/rigid abdomen, vomiting blood or black stools, jaundice with rigors or confusion, or faintness/shock develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The real risk is UNDER-action — mislabelling as another gallstone attack or gastritis and managing at home, rather than escalating a febrile surgical abdomen.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Tokyo Guidelines (TG18) for acute cholecystitis and acute cholangitis
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Diagnose acute cholecystitis from local signs of inflammation (Murphy's sign, RUQ pain/mass), systemic signs (fever, raised inflammatory markers) and imaging findings; grade severity and treat with antibiotics, supportive care and cholecystectomy.
